coredata.dc:title: In situ decoration of CuSCN nanorod arrays with carbon quantum dots for highly efficient photoelectrochemical performance
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Fang
Chen, Da
Hu, Zijun
Qin, Laishun
Sun, Xingguo
Huang, Yuexiang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the in situ decoration of CuSCN nanorod thin films with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was achieved through a one-pot electrochemical deposition process. The hybridization of CQDs would not change the nanorod structure and p-type characteristic of CuSCN thin film. Compared with the pristine CuSCN thin film, the CQDs/CuSCN composite thin film exhibited significantly improved photoelectrochemical (PEC) activities. The photocurrent intensity of the composite thin film electrode was approximately 6.5 times that of the CuSCN thin film electrode. The improved PEC activities could be ascribed to the highly efficient generation, separation and migration of excited charge carriers derived from the decoration of CQDs on the surface of CuSCN nanorods, as verified by the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS), photoluminescence, charge extraction, intensity-modulated photocurrent spectroscopy (IMPS) and intensity-modulated photovoltage spectroscopy (IMVS) measurements. The superior PEC properties of the CQDs/CuSCN composite thin film make it a promising hole transporting material or photocathode material for practical PEC applications including solar cells and PEC water splitting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CuSCN nanorods
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Electrochemical deposition
Photoelectrochemical performance

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51572250 , 51372237 ), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LY17E020009 ), Research Project of Public Welfare Quality Testing Industry of China (No. 201510072 ), and CAS Key Laboratory of Design and Assembly of Functional Nanostructures , Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure of Matter , Chinese Academy of Sciences , China (No. 2013DP173231 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the CuSCN thin film and CQDs/CuSCN composite thin film electrochemically deposited on FTO substrate; (B) FESEM images of the CuSCN thin film (B) and CQDs/CuSCN composite thin film (C) electrochemically deposited on FTO substrate; EDS mapping image of carbon element (D) for the CQDs/CuSCN composite thin film electrochemically deposited on FTO substrate. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

TEM and HRTEM images of the electrodeposited CuSCN (A, B, C) and CQDs/CuSCN (D, E, F) layers, which were peeled off from the FTO substrate. The insets in images (C) and (F) are the corresponding SAED patterns of the electrodeposited CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN layers.

(A) Mott-Schottky plots of the CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN thin film electrochemically deposited on FTO substrate; (B) Photocurrent responses of the CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN thin film electrochemically deposited on FTO substrate with light on/off cycles at 0 V vs SCE in 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) EIS spectra for the prepared CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN thin film electrodes in 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution upon a white LED light irradiation (the inset is the equivalent circuit used to fit the impedance data); (B) PL emission spectra of the prepared CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN thin film at an excitation wavelength of 394 nm; (C) Extracted charge (obtained by integrating the short circuit current after a certain decay time) as a function of decay time for the prepared CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN thin film electrodes. (Inset: Open circuit (OC) and short circuit (SC) sequence showing the relationship between the illumination and the measurement of potential and charge in the charge extraction experiments.). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) IMPS and (B) IMVS plots of the prepared CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN thin film electrodes in 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution upon a white LED light irradiation; (C) Schematic illustration of the highly efficient charge transfer and separation process within the prepared CQDs/CuSCN thin film electrode in comparison with the prepared CuSCN thin film electrode for photoelectrochemical (PEC) reaction. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The obtained R
ct values and IMPS/IMVS parameters for the prepared CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN thin film electrodes.

head.ce:title: In situ decoration of CuSCN nanorod arrays with carbon quantum dots for highly efficient photoelectrochemical performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, China Jiliang University, Hangzhou 310018, Zhejiang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the in situ decoration of CuSCN nanorod thin films with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was achieved through a one-pot electrochemical deposition process. The hybridization of CQDs would not change the nanorod structure and p-type characteristic of CuSCN thin film. Compared with the pristine CuSCN thin film, the CQDs/CuSCN composite thin film exhibited significantly improved photoelectrochemical (PEC) activities. The photocurrent intensity of the composite thin film electrode was approximately 6.5 times that of the CuSCN thin film electrode. The improved PEC activities could be ascribed to the highly efficient generation, separation and migration of excited charge carriers derived from the decoration of CQDs on the surface of CuSCN nanorods, as verified by the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS), photoluminescence, charge extraction, intensity-modulated photocurrent spectroscopy (IMPS) and intensity-modulated photovoltage spectroscopy (IMVS) measurements. The superior PEC properties of the CQDs/CuSCN composite thin film make it a promising hole transporting material or photocathode material for practical PEC applications including solar cells and PEC water splitting.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, p-type semiconductor thin films have attracted much attention because of their promising applications in a wide range of fields, such as light emitting diode (LED) [1], photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting [2], dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) [3,4], extremely thin absorber (ETA) solar cells [5], and perovskite solar cells (PSCs) [4,6]. Among these p-type thin films, CuSCN thin films are particularly of interest. CuSCN possesses a wide band gap of 3.6 eV [7], high transparency, suitable band gap level position [8], decent hole conductivity (≥5 × 10−4 S cm−1) [1], and chemical stability [9] as well as low cost. Thus, p-type CuSCN thin films have many attractive features as a photocathode material for PEC applications, and have recently found wide application as a solid hole-transporting electrolyte in replace of liquid electrolyte in DSSCs [10], ETA solar cells [11] and PSCs [12]. In this regard, many of the current issues occurring in the liquid electrolyte-based solar cells, such as easy leakage, difficult sealing and poor stability, can be solved, though the performances of p-CuSCN-based solid state solar cells are not comparable with those of liquid electrolyte solar cells and still needs improvement. Therefore, it is still a great challenge to modify p-type CuSCN thin films for improving their PEC performances.

To date, much effort has been made to regulate the PEC properties of p-type CuSCN thin films, with focuses on the following two strategies. Firstly, the PEC properties of CuSCN thin films can be regulated by controlling the stoichiometric ratio of SCN− in CuSCN. Different stoichiometric ratios of SCN−/Cu+ in CuSCN thin films would generally result in entirely different PEC activities, which is considered as a consequence of composition defects or self-doping in CuSCN thin films [7,13,14]. For example, Perera et al. [13] have exposed solid CuSCN to halogen gases such as Cl2 or to a solution of (SCN)2 in CCl4 and thereby doped the semiconductor with (SCN)2 to improve the conductivity of CuSCN. By using such a doped CuSCN thin film, the conversion efficiency (η) performance of the solar cell was considerably enhanced from 0.75% without SCN doping to 2.39% after SCN doping. Secondly, another effective way to regulate the PEC properties of CuSCN thin films is to control their morphological structure. CuSCN thin films of different morphological structures can be prepared by using a variety of methods, such as dip-coating or spray coating of CuSCN organic solution [13,15], chemical bath deposition [16], successive ionic adsorption and reaction [16,17], and electrochemical deposition [18–21]. In contrast to those particulate thin films, CuSCN thin films with one-dimensional morphological structure (such as nanowires, nanorods) have attracted much attention owing to their high surface areas, relatively good charge transport properties, and special light-trapping effect, which can improve the light absorption and facilitate the generation, transfer and separation of photoinduced carriers thus leading to the enhanced PEC properties of CuSCN thin films. For example, Huang et al. [20] has recently reported the influence of annealing temperature on structural, optical, electrical and PEC characteristics of electrodeposited CuSCN thin films. It was found that the crystallographic orientation would change, and the optical energy band gap of thin films slightly increased with increasing annealing temperatures. Moreover, the CuSCN films annealed at 400 °C exhibited much better PEC performance than the as-deposited CuSCN film. Thus, it can be seen that the above two strategies can to some extent boost the PEC characteristics of CuSCN thin films by adjusting their composition and structure, but the boosting effect is not obvious. In consequence, it is of great concern to develop alternative ways to efficiently improve the PEC properties of CuSCN thin films.

As well-known, a promising strategy to enhance the PEC performance of a photocatalyst is to fabricate semiconductor heterojunctions, which can simultaneously optimize light absorption and promote charge separation at the interface between the two semiconductors [22]. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are predominantly composed of graphitic carbon (sp2 carbon) with a size below 10 nm [23], have been widely used to couple with semiconductor nanostructures, such as Cu2O microspheres [24], TiO2 nanostructures [25,26], BiVO4 nanostructures [27], g-C3N4 [28] and Cu2S nanowire arrays [29], by which the photocatalytic and PEC properties can be remarkably enhanced due to the quantum size effect, broad band optical absorption and high electrical conductivity of CQDs [30]. Enlightened by this, we attempt to markedly improve the PEC performance of CuSCN thin films by the coupling of CQDs. To the best of our knowledge, there is no research on the CQDs-decorated CuSCN thin films in association with their PEC performances.

Herein, CQDs were decorated in situ on the surface of CuSCN nanorod array thin films through a facile one-pot electrochemical deposition process. Various techniques were used to characterize the morphological and structural features of the CQDs/CuSCN composite thin films, and their PEC activities were then evaluated by using different electrochemical or PEC measurements. After the decoration of CQDs, the PEC activities of the CuSCN thin film were greatly improved compared with pristine CuSCN nanorod array thin film. The superior PEC properties of the CQDs/CuSCN composite thin film make it a promising hole transporting material or photocathode material for practical PEC applications, such as DSSCs, PSCs, ETA solar cells and PEC water splitting.

All chemicals were of analytical grade without further purification. The colloidal solution of CQDs was firstly synthesized by a similar hydrothermal synthesis process as reported elsewhere [31]. Typically, 1.54 g of citric acid was dissolved in 80 mL of deionized water under vigorous stirring to form a transparent solution. The solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 24 h. When the reactor was cooled down to room temperature naturally, the resulting colloidal solution containing CQDs was then obtained after centrifugation and dialysis.

The CQDs/CuSCN composite thin films were prepared via a facile one-pot electrochemical deposition process. The electrochemical deposition was carried out on a CHI-660 E electrochemical working station in a conventional three-electrode electrochemical cell using an FTO conductive glass as the working electrode, a platinum plate as the counter electrode, and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. The aqueous electrolyte solution containing 0.012 M CuSO4·5H2O, 0.012 M ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) and 0.003 M KSCN was enriched with a dispersion of 5 mg mL−1 CQDs. Prior to the electrochemical deposition, the solution was deaerated by thoroughly stirring. The CQDs-decorated CuSCN nanorod layers were grown potentiostatically on the FTO surface at a cathodic potential of −0.4 V with total passed charge of 40 mC/cm2. The deposited thin films were rinsed with purified water and dried in air at room temperature. For comparison, the CuSCN nanorod array thin film was also prepared by using the same procedure as mentioned above without the addition of CQDs in the electrolyte solution.

The crystalline structure of the obtained thin films was characterized by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) using a Bruker D2 X-ray diffractometer equipped with Cu K
α radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm). The morphological features of the films were analyzed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU 8010) attached with energy dispersive X-ray Spectroscope (EDS). The microstructures were also observed by a field-emission transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The absorbance measurement was employed to characterize the optical properties of the films by using an UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-3600) and using an identical FTO-coated glass substrate as the reference. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on a Hitachi High-Tech F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer with a 450 W xenon lamp as an excitation source (excitation wavelength λ = 394 nm).

The PEC tests were conducted on a Zennium potentiostat/galvanostat (CIMPS-2, ZAHNER-elektrik GmbH & Co. KG, Germany) in a three-electrode configuration with a quartz window, where the obtained CQDs/CuSCN or CuSCN thin film as the working photoelectrode, a SCE electrode as the reference electrode, and Pt plate as the counter electrode in 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. The photoelectrode sample with an identical reactive area of 1.0 cm2 was placed in the electrochemical cell at a distance of 5.0 cm from the quartz window. All measurements were performed in a screened dark box, and care was taken to eliminate stray light. The cells were illuminated from the substrate side with a high intensity white light emitting diode (LED WLC02, ZAHNER-elektrik GmbH & Co. KG, Germany), and the intensity was calibrated to 100 mW/cm2. The photocurrent measurements of all the samples were recorded under chopped light irradiation by controlling the LED light source using a LED driver (PP211, ZAHNER-elektrik GmbH & Co. KG, Germany) that synchronically allowed the switching of the cell between light-on and light-off state. For the intensity-modulated photocurrent spectroscopy (IMPS) and intensity-modulated photovoltage spectroscopy (IMVS) measurements, modulated illumination was provided by a high intensity white LED controlled by a LED driver (PP211) that allowed the superimposition of sinusoidal modulation (∼10%) on a dc illumination level. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were recorded in the same three-electrode configuration by applying an AC voltage with 5 mV amplitude in the frequency range from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz upon a white LED irradiation. Mott-Schottky analysis was carried out at an applied frequency of 1000 Hz in the dark from 0.0 V to +0.5 V. The number of excited charge carriers in the prepared thin films was determined by charge extraction method [31–33]. Initially, the cell was at open circuit potential (OCP) under darkness during 20 s; after that, it was suddenly illuminated during 20 s, and then the light was interrupted. After a certain period of decay time at OCP (from 0 to 1000 ms), the cell was short-circuited under darkness to quickly extract all of the charge carriers as a current transient. The integral of the current transient could yield the number of charge carriers stored in the thin film photoelectrode.

The colloidal solution of CQDs was successfully synthesized by hydrothermal method, as demonstrated in Fig. S1. Fig. S1(A) shows the TEM image of as-prepared CQDs, revealing that the product consisted of very small particles with an average particle size of 3–5 nm, which were well separated from each other. From Fig. S1(B), the PL emission peak (when excited at 360 nm) centered at around 436 nm and a blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm) of CQD solution were observed, which was consistent with the previously-reported work [34,35], further confirming the successful preparation of CQDs.

The crystal structure of the prepared CQDs/CuSCN and CuSCN thin films were characterized by XRD measurements, and the results are shown in Fig. 1 (A). The diffraction peaks for the electrodeposited CuSCN thin film could be indexed to the rhombohedral phase of β-CuSCN (JCPDS 29-0581) [20,36]. For the CQDs/CuSCN thin film, the diffraction peaks were similar to that of CuSCN, suggesting the negligible effect on crystalline structure with the decoration of CQDs probably because of poor crystallinity of CQDs. The XRD patterns of the powders of electrodeposited CuSCN nanorods and CQDs/CuSCN composite nanorods split from the FTO substrate (Fig. S2) were well matched with single standard JCPDS data (JCPDS 29-0581), further confirming the crystalline rhombohedral β-CuSCN in both samples. Fig. 1(B) and (C) present the SEM images of the CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN composite thin film electrodeposited on the FTO conductive glass substrate. As seen in Fig. 1(B), the CuSCN thin film displayed a vertical and high density alignment of one-dimensional nanorods on FTO substrate, with well-defined faces and a relatively uniform diameter of about 80 nm. For the CQDs/CuSCN composite thin film (Fig. 1(C)), the morphological structure was also in nanorod form with a similar diameter of ∼80 nm, indicating that the decoration of CQDs would not change the nanorod structure of CuSCN. However, the surface of the resulting nanorods in composite thin film became blurred because of the uniform distribution of CQDs on the surface of CuSCN nanorods, as verified by the carbon elemental mapping (Fig. 1(D)).

The microstructures of the electrodeposited CuSCN nanorods and CQDs-decorated CuSCN nanorods were further investigated by TEM and HRTEM images. As shown in Fig. 2 (A) and (B), the electrodeposited CuSCN layer exhibited a relatively uniform nanorod structure in shape with an intact smooth surface, an average diameter of 70–90 nm and a length of several hundred nanometers, which was in accordance with the SEM result. The CQDs/CuSCN composite layer (Fig. 2(D) and (E)) maintained the nanorod geometry with a rough surface because of the even distribution of CQDs on the surface of CuSCN nanorods. This clearly reveals that the CQDs have been successfully decorated on the surfaces of CuSCN nanorods as the nanoparticle sizes were consistent with that of the pristine CQDs (Fig. S1(A)), and the heterostructure could be also formed at the interface between CQDs and CuSCN nanorods. Moreover, the HRTEM images of the CuSCN nanorods and CQDs/CuSCN composites (Fig. 2(C) and (F)) identified an interplanar spacing (∼0.33 nm) of the crystal lattice fringes corresponding to the (101) plane of the rhombohedral phase of CuSCN, and the diffraction spots ((003), (101) and (113) in their representative selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns (the insets in Fig. 2(C) and (F)) further manifested the polycrystalline character of the electrodeposited CuSCN nanorods and CQDs/CuSCN composite nanorods. Meanwhile, from the HRTEM image of the CQDs/CuSCN composite (Fig. 2(F)), the intimate contact at the heterostructure interface between CQDs and CuSCN nanorod in the composite was also verified, and no lattice fringes were observed in the CQDs decorated on the surface of CuSCN nanorods, indicating that the decorated CQDs were amorphous carbon. In addition, the decoration of CQDs on the surface of CuSCN nanorods could significantly improve the light absorption activity in the visible region, as shown in the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. S3). The enhanced light absorption properties would to some extent contribute to the improved PEC performance of the composite thin film.

As illustrated in Fig. 3 (A), the negative slopes of the Mott–Schottky plots confirms the p-type characteristic of the prepared CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN thin films, implying the decoration of CQDs would not change the p-type semiconductor nature of CuSCN thin film. The flat-band potentials of CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN, calculated from the x-intercepts of the linear region, were found to be 0.30 V and 0.20 V vs. SCE, respectively. For a p-type semiconductor, the E fb lies very close to the top of the valance band (VB) [37], and thus the VBs are roughly estimated as the same as the flat-band potentials for easy comparison. That is, the estimated VB value of CQDs/CuSCN thin film was ca. 0.20 V, which was more negative than that of CuSCN thin film by ca. 0.10 V. It could be inferred that the electronic interactions between CQDs and CuSCN caused a negative shift in the VB potential, which would facilitate the charge separation in PEC application probably because of the negatively-shifted VB position [38]. To validate the beneficial effect of the decorated CQDs on the PEC activity of CuSCN, the transient photocurrent responses of the prepared CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN thin films were measured via several on/off cycles upon a white LED irradiation. As shown in Fig. 3(B), the photocurrent with good reproducibility, stability and reversibility for both samples could be attained during six on/off cycles of measurement, manifesting that both CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN thin films could respond quickly to UV–vis irradiation. Specifically, the photocurrent of CQDs/CuSCN thin film was approximately 6.5 times that of CuSCN, confirming that the decoration of CQDs could significantly improve the PEC activity of CuSCN thin film. The enhanced photocurrent of the CQDs/CuSCN thin film indicates a much more efficient photoinduced charge separation and transfer process in the composite thin film. In addition, the dependence of the enhanced PEC activities of the prepared CQDs/CuSCN composite thin film electrode on the applied bias potential was also verified. Fig. S4 illustrates the linear sweep voltammogram (LSV) curves of the electrodeposited CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN thin film electrodes. For both electrodes, the cathodic currents in the dark could be neglected, whereas upon illumination considerable photocurrents were generated and increased gradually with the applied bias potential. Compared to the prepared CuSCN thin film electrode, the CQDs/CuSCN thin film electrode displayed a much higher photocurrent at the same bias potential, confirming the improvement of PEC activities by the decoration of CQDs. Moreover, the photocurrent onset for the CQDs/CuSCN thin film electrode (0.082 V vs. SCE) was a little more positive than that of the CuSCN thin film electrode (0.056 V vs. SCE), which might be due to the elevated VB position of CQDs/CuSCN as demonstrated in Mott-Schottky analysis. The positively-shifted onset potential of the CQDs/CuSCN composite thin film electrode would be probably beneficial to produce the cathodic photocurrent, thus leading to the enhanced PEC activity of CQDs/CuSCN composite electrode.

To elucidate the boosted PEC mechanism of the prepared CQDs/CuSCN thin film, the separation, migration, and recombination process of photogenerated charges in the CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN thin films were investigated by various PEC techniques including EIS, PL and charge extraction measurements. Fig. 4 (A) shows the EIS spectra of the prepared CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN thin films. For a given EIS spectrum, the smaller arc radius generally implies a more effective separation of photogenerated electron–hole pairs and a higher efficiency of charge transfer across the electrode/electrolyte interface [39]. As shown, both spectra were composed of a depressed semicircle in the high-to-medium frequency range and a straight line in the low frequency range, which was analyzed by using an equivalent circuit (inset in Fig. 4(A)) where R
s , R
ct , CPE and W are the solution resistance, the charge transfer resistance, the double layer capacitance, and the Warburg impedance, respectively. The electrochemical parameters of the two samples were simulated by Z-view software, and the fitted data obtained from the equivalent circuit were well consistent with the experimental results. It is clearly revealed that the CQDs/CuSCN composite electrode exhibited a much smaller charge-transfer resistance (R ct) (17.4 kΩ) than the bare CuSCN electrode (23.2 kΩ) (Table 1 ), confirming that the decoration of CQDs would facilitate the separation and migration of photogenerated charges in the composite thin film, which was in agreement with the photocurrent result. In addition, the recombination of excited electrons and holes can be reflected from the PL emission intensity, and the lower emission intensity generally means a decrease in recombination probability [40]. Fig. 4(B) shows the PL spectra of the prepared CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN thin films at an excitation wavelength of 394 nm. It can be seen that the CuSCN thin film exhibited four strong characteristic emission peaks centered at 392 nm, 490 nm, 592 nm and 684 nm, respectively, which could be generally assigned to near-band-edge emission and defect and surface states [40,41]. For the CQDs/CuSCN thin film, the four characteristic emission peaks were still retained, but the emission intensities were decreased, indicating a much lower recombination probability of excited electrons and holes in the composite thin film which would contribute significantly to the enhanced PEC activity. Furthermore, charge extraction measurements were performed to estimate the number of photogenerated charge carriers accumulated in the prepared CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN thin films, which could intuitively reflect their PEC activities. Fig. 4(C) shows the extracted charge as a function of decay time for the prepared CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN thin film electrodes. As seen, the number of charge carriers extracted from the CQDs/CuSCN thin film without decay (t = 0) was nearly 3.4 times that of the CuSCN thin film, verifying that the decoration of CQDs would greatly facilitate the generation of excited charge carriers in the CuSCN thin film thus to improve their PEC activity. Meanwhile, the measured charge carriers extracted from the CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN thin films as a function of decay time show a first-order decay reaction kinetics, and the relationship between charge density (Q) and decay time (t) can be fitted with a simple exponential function as follows [33]:
(1)
Q
( t )
=
A 0
+
A n
e
− t /
τ n
where τ
n is the apparent lifetime of charge carriers. The τ
n data obtained from the CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN thin films were 72 and 122 ms, respectively. Apparently, the enhanced apparent charge lifetime of the CQDs/CuSCN thin film would benefit the separation and transfer of excited charge carriers, which could also make a great contribution to the improved PEC activities.

In addition, IMPS and IMVS were also conducted to further explore the influence of the decoration of CQDs on the kinetic behaviors of photoinduced charge carriers of CuSCN thin film electrode. As discussed elsewhere [42,43], the lifetime of charge carriers (τ
n ) and the mean transit time (τ
d ) for photogenerated charge carriers can be estimated from the frequency at the minimal value in the IMVS and IMPS plots by setting τ
n = (2πf min(IMVS))−1 and τ
d = (2πf min(IMPS))−1, respectively. Fig. 5 (A) and (B) shows the typical IMPS and IMVS plots of the prepared CuSCN and CQDs/CuSCN thin film electrodes, and the corresponding lifetime of charge carriers and mean transit time for photogenerated charge carriers were summarized in Table 1.

It can be seen that the CQDs/CuSCN thin film electrode gave much longer charge lifetime (∼108.32 ms) than the CuSCN thin film electrode (∼66.45 ms), which was in consistent with those data obtained from charge extraction measurements. For the CQDs/CuSCN thin film electrode, the photogenerated charge carriers could be collected more effectively before they recombine in the PEC process if they had a longer lifetime, and the longer charge lifetime suggests that more charge carriers surviving from the back reaction could contribute to the improvement of the photocurrent. Moreover, the mean transit time of photogenerated charge carriers from the CQDs/CuSCN thin film electrode was only 2.07 ms, which was also much shorter than that from the CuSCN thin film electrode (∼3.31 ms), confirming the much more efficient separation of photogenerated charge carriers within the CQDs/CuSCN thin film electrode when comparing with the CuSCN thin film electrode. The more efficient charge transfer and separation within the CQDs/CuSCN thin film electrode would suppress the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers and thus promote the PEC activities.

Combining the results of EIS, PL, charge extraction, IMPS and IMVS measurements, it can be inferred that the formation of the heterostructure between CQDs and CuSCN nanorods should be responsible for more efficient separation and migration of excited charge carriers, and thus the enhanced PEC activity of the composite thin film. Fig. 5(C) schematically illustrates the efficient charge transfer and separation process within the prepared CQDs/CuSCN thin film electrode in comparison with the prepared CuSCN thin film electrode. Upon irradiation, the CQDs/CuSCN thin film could be excited to produce photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Owing to the easy recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs and the sluggish charge transport within the CuSCN thin film electrode, only few photogenerated charge carriers could reach the surface of CuSCN thin film to participate in the PEC reactions, thus leading to poor PEC activities. When CQDs were decorated on the surface of CuSCN thin film, the heterojunction could be constructed at the interface between CQDs and CuSCN nanorods. Under this circumstance, the photogenerated electrons could be efficiently transferred from the conduction band (CB) of CuSCN to the surface of CQDs, which could facilitate the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs thus leading to the enhanced PEC activity of the CQDs/CuSCN thin film electrode.

In summary, the in situ decoration of CQDs on the surface of CuSCN thin film was achieved through a one-pot electrodeposition process. The prepared CQDs/CuSCN composite thin film still maintained one-dimensional nanorod array structure and the p-type characteristic of the pristine CuSCN thin film, and CQDs with a particle size of 3–5 nm were evenly distributed on the surface of CuSCN nanorods. After the decoration of CQDs, the PEC activity of CuSCN thin film was significantly improved, which could be attributed to the efficient generation, separation and migration of excited charge carriers in the composite thin film, as verified by EIS, PL, charge extraction, IMPS and IMVS measurements. Thus, the present work provides a simple alternative method to effectively improve the PEC activities of p-type CuSCN thin film through the decoration of CQDs. The importance of this study also lies in the fact that it reveals the possibility of expanding the related work from p-CuSCN thin film to other p-type semiconductor thin film for developing highly efficient hole-transporting p-type thin films with promising applications in solar energy conversion, photocatalysis, and sensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51572250, 51372237), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LY17E020009), Research Project of Public Welfare Quality Testing Industry of China (No. 201510072), and CAS Key Laboratory of Design and Assembly of Functional Nanostructures, Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure of Matter, Chinese Academy of Sciences, China (No. 2013DP173231).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.09.073.
